back: A constraint between two sets of attributes in a relation where for each value of attribute A, there is a set of values for attribute B and a set of values for attribute C, but these sets are independent of each other.
A relation is in 4NF if it is in BCNF and contains no non-trivial multivalued dependencies. In other words, each relation should contain only one independent multi-valued fact.
A MVD A ⇢⇢ B is trivial if either B ⊆ A or A ∪ B = all attributes of the relation. Trivial MVDs are always satisfied and do not cause redundancy.
A MVD A ⇢⇢ B is non-trivial if B is not a subset of A and A ∪ B is not equal to all attributes of the relation. Non-trivial MVDs cause redundancy and require decomposition.

front: Difference between Functional Dependency and Multivalued Dependency
back: FD specifies that for one value of A, there is exactly one value of B (A→B). MVD specifies that for one value of A, there are multiple values of B that are independent of other attributes.
A relation R satisfies join dependency (R1, R2, ..., Rn) if R can be reconstructed by joining the projections R1, R2, ..., Rn. 4NF eliminates MVDs which are a special case of join dependencies.
An alternative name for multivalued dependency. It generates additional tuples in the relation when a tuple with certain values exists.
BCNF eliminates redundancy from functional dependencies but cannot handle situations where attributes have independent multi-values. 4NF addresses this additional type of dependency.

front: What is the lossless-join decomposition for MVD?
back: A decomposition of R into R1 and R2 is lossless if (R1 ∩ R2) ⇢⇢ R1 or (R1 ∩ R2) ⇢⇢ R2 holds. This ensures no information is lost when rejoining decomposed relations.

front: What is dependency preservation in decomposition?
back: It means all original dependencies (FDs and MVDs) can be enforced using the decomposed relations without requiring joins. 4NF decomposition may not always preserve dependencies.

front: Example of a relation requiring 4NF decomposition
back: STUDENT(StudentID, Course, Hobby) where StudentID ⇢⇢ Course and StudentID ⇢⇢ Hobby. This creates redundant rows as courses and hobbies are independent.

front: How to decompose a relation to 4NF?
back: For each non-trivial MVD A ⇢⇢ B in relation R, decompose R into (A ∪ B) and (R - (B - A)). Repeat until no non-trivial MVDs remain.

front: What is the complement rule for MVDs?
back: If A ⇢⇢ B holds in R, then A ⇢⇢ (R - B) also holds. This means if A determines a set of values for B, it also determines values for all other attributes.
Not always. 4NF decomposition may lead to loss of dependency preservation. Sometimes we accept some redundancy to keep the schema simpler and avoid joins.
If A ⇢⇢ B holds and X ⊆ Y, then AY ⇢⇢ BX also holds. Adding attributes to both sides preserves the MVD.
